text: Robins sing at dawn across North America.
Blue Jays mimic hawk calls to scare other birds.
Cardinals do not migrate and sing year-round.
Hummingbirds can fly backward and hover in place.
Owls can rotate their heads nearly all the way around.
Peregrine Falcons dive at speeds over two hundred miles per hour.
Crows can recognize and remember human faces for years.
Woodpeckers drum on trees to find insects hiding in bark.
Penguins waddle on ice but swim gracefully underwater.
Flamingos get their pink color from the shrimp they eat.
Pelicans scoop fish from the water with their large bills.
Swallows catch insects in mid-flight with acrobatic turns.
Eagles have eyesight eight times sharper than a human.
Parrots can learn to mimic hundreds of human words.
Sparrows take dust baths to clean their feathers.
Geese fly in a V formation to conserve energy.
Toucans use their large bills to regulate body temperature.
Starlings gather in massive flocks called murmurations.
Kiwis are flightless birds that hunt using smell.
Roadrunners sprint across the desert at twenty miles per hour.
Kingfishers dive headfirst into water to catch fish.
Mockingbirds can sing over two hundred different songs.
Hawks circle high above open fields searching for prey.
Ostriches are the fastest birds on land, running at forty-five miles per hour.
Puffins carry multiple fish crosswise in their colorful bills.
Herons stand perfectly still in shallow water waiting for fish to swim past.
Wrens are tiny but have one of the loudest songs relative to their body size.
Magpies are one of the few animals that can recognize themselves in a mirror.
Vultures soar for hours without flapping by riding columns of rising warm air.
Swifts spend almost their entire lives in the air, even sleeping on the wing.
The American Robin is one of the most familiar birds in North America. It is found in gardens, parks, and forests across the continent. Robins are known for their cheerful song and orange-red breast.
Blue Jays are intelligent and adaptable birds found throughout eastern North America. They are known for their striking blue plumage, crest, and loud calls. Blue Jays can mimic the calls of hawks.

The Northern Cardinal is a beloved songbird found across eastern North America. Males are brilliant red with a black face mask. Cardinals do not migrate and can be seen year-round at backyard feeders.

Bald Eagles are powerful birds of prey found near large bodies of open water throughout North America. They build the largest nests of any bird, sometimes reaching ten feet across and weighing over a ton.

The Ruby-throated Hummingbird is the only hummingbird that breeds in eastern North America. Despite weighing less than a nickel, these tiny birds migrate across the Gulf of Mexico each spring and fall.

Great Horned Owls are fierce predators found across the Americas. They have excellent hearing and vision, allowing them to hunt in total darkness. Their deep hooting call is a familiar sound of the night.

The Peregrine Falcon is the fastest animal on Earth, reaching speeds over 240 miles per hour during hunting dives. These remarkable birds have recovered from near extinction thanks to conservation efforts.

Cedar Waxwings are sleek songbirds with silky plumage and distinctive red wax-like tips on their wing feathers. They travel in large flocks and feed primarily on berries and small fruits throughout the year.

The Barn Owl has a heart-shaped face that helps funnel sound to its ears. It can locate and capture prey in complete darkness using hearing alone. Barn Owls are found on every continent except Antarctica.

Red-tailed Hawks are the most common hawks in North America. They soar on broad wings over open fields and along roadsides. Their distinctive raspy scream is often used in movies to represent all birds of prey.
The Painted Bunting is one of the most colorful birds in North America. Males display brilliant blue, green, and red plumage. Despite their vivid colors, they can be difficult to spot in dense brush.

Sandhill Cranes are tall gray birds that perform elaborate dancing displays during courtship. They mate for life and can live for over twenty years. Their trumpeting calls can be heard from more than a mile away.

The Common Loon is known for its haunting calls that echo across northern lakes. These diving birds can stay underwater for up to five minutes while pursuing fish. They need a long runway of water to take off.

Snowy Owls breed on the Arctic tundra and sometimes appear far south during winter irruptions. Unlike most owls, they are active during the day. Their white plumage provides camouflage in snowy landscapes.

The Pileated Woodpecker is the largest woodpecker in North America. It creates rectangular holes in trees while searching for carpenter ants. Its loud drumming and distinctive call echo through mature forests.

The Belted Kingfisher hunts by plunging headfirst into water to catch fish. Unlike most birds, the female is more colorful than the male, sporting an extra rusty band across her belly. They nest in burrows dug into earthen banks.

Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities near water. The day-old ducklings leap from the nest, sometimes falling fifty feet to the ground below, and bounce unharmed before following their mother to the nearest pond.

The Black-capped Chickadee is a small, bold bird that readily visits backyard feeders. It has an excellent memory and can remember thousands of hiding spots where it stores seeds. Its cheerful song brightens winter days.

Ospreys are remarkable fishermen that hover over water before plunging feet-first to grab fish with their sharp talons. They have a reversible outer toe and barbed foot pads that help them grip slippery prey.

Atlantic Puffins spend most of their lives at sea and return to coastal cliffs only to breed. They can hold a dozen or more small fish in their bills at once, arranged neatly in a row. Their colorful beaks fade to gray during winter.

The Great Blue Heron is the largest heron in North America. It stands motionless in shallow water for long periods before striking with lightning speed to catch fish. Despite its size, it weighs only about five pounds thanks to hollow bones.

American Goldfinches are among the last birds to nest each summer, waiting until thistles produce seeds and down. Males molt from drab olive to brilliant yellow each spring. They are strict vegetarians, feeding almost entirely on seeds.

The Northern Mockingbird can learn and perform over two hundred songs during its lifetime. Males sing both day and night, especially during breeding season. Each song is typically repeated three to five times before switching to the next.

Turkey Vultures have an exceptional sense of smell that allows them to locate carrion hidden beneath the forest canopy. They soar for hours on thermals without flapping, tilting from side to side in a distinctive wobbly flight.

The Indigo Bunting navigates during its nocturnal migration by reading the pattern of stars in the night sky. Males appear brilliant blue in sunlight but are actually black, and their color comes entirely from the way light refracts through their feathers.

Anna's Hummingbird is one of the few hummingbirds that does not migrate. Males perform spectacular courtship dives, climbing high into the air and then plummeting earthward at speeds that produce a loud chirp from their tail feathers.

Brown Pelicans plunge-dive from heights of sixty feet to catch fish near the surface. Air sacs beneath their skin cushion the impact of hitting the water. They were once endangered due to pesticides but have made a remarkable recovery.

The Roseate Spoonbill wades through shallow water sweeping its flat, spoon-shaped bill from side to side to filter out small fish and crustaceans. Its vivid pink plumage comes from the carotenoid pigments in the shrimp and other creatures it eats.

Clark's Nutcracker buries up to 98,000 pine seeds each autumn across thousands of locations. It can remember the precise location of most of these caches months later, even under deep snow. Seeds it forgets often sprout into new trees.

The American Dipper is the only songbird in North America that swims. It walks along the bottom of fast-flowing mountain streams to catch aquatic insects. A special oil keeps its feathers waterproof and it can even walk underwater against the current.

Resplendent Quetzals were sacred to the ancient Maya and Aztec civilizations. Males grow spectacular tail feathers that can reach three feet in length during breeding season. They nest in rotted-out holes in tree trunks high in cloud forests.

The Shoebill is a massive bird that stands nearly five feet tall in the swamps of central Africa. It hunts by standing completely still, then lunging forward to grab lungfish and even small crocodiles in its enormous shoe-shaped bill.

Kakapos are flightless parrots from New Zealand and one of the longest-lived birds in the world. They are nocturnal, have a strong pleasant smell, and males attract mates by inflating a throat sac and producing a deep booming call that carries for miles.

The European Robin is a small bird with a bright orange breast found across Europe and into parts of Asia. Despite its cheerful appearance, it is fiercely territorial and will aggressively confront other robins that enter its domain.

Secretary Birds are tall raptors that hunt on foot across the African savanna. They kill venomous snakes by stomping on them with powerful legs that can deliver kicks with a force five times their own body weight.

The Hoatzin is a peculiar bird found in the Amazon basin. Its chicks have claws on their wings that they use to climb trees, a feature reminiscent of ancient dinosaur ancestors. Adults have a unique digestive system that ferments leaves like a cow.

Bee-eaters are colorful birds that specialize in catching bees and wasps in flight. Before eating their prey, they beat the insect against a branch to remove the stinger. Some species nest in large colonies dug into sandy riverbanks.

The Harpy Eagle is one of the most powerful raptors in the world, with talons as large as a grizzly bear's claws. It hunts monkeys and sloths in the rainforest canopy. Despite its enormous strength, it can weave through dense trees with surprising agility.

Frigatebirds are large seabirds that cannot walk well on land or swim. They survive by snatching fish from the ocean's surface in flight and by harassing other seabirds until they drop their catch. Males inflate a bright red throat pouch to attract mates.

The Kea is a large parrot native to the mountains of New Zealand's South Island. It is known for its exceptional intelligence, playful behavior, and curiosity. Keas have been observed solving complex puzzles, using tools, and even working together in teams.

The California Condor is one of the rarest birds in the world and the largest flying land bird in North America, with a wingspan reaching nearly ten feet. By 1987, all remaining wild condors were captured for a breeding program after their population dropped to just 27 individuals. Thanks to decades of conservation work, captive breeding, and careful reintroduction, their numbers have grown to over five hundred birds. Condors are scavengers that can soar for miles without flapping their wings, using thermal updrafts to glide effortlessly over mountains and valleys.

The Arctic Tern holds the record for the longest migration of any animal on Earth, traveling roughly 44,000 miles each year from its Arctic breeding grounds to the Antarctic and back again. This remarkable journey means the Arctic Tern experiences two summers per year and sees more daylight than any other creature on the planet. These small but tough seabirds can live for over thirty years, meaning a single tern may fly the equivalent of three round trips to the moon during its lifetime. They nest in large colonies on rocky shores and fiercely defend their eggs by diving at any intruder that comes too close.

The Superb Lyrebird of southeastern Australia is widely considered one of the most impressive mimics in the entire animal kingdom. Males construct elaborate display mounds on the forest floor where they perform for potential mates by fanning their extraordinary tail feathers into a shimmering silver canopy over their bodies. During these displays, they produce an astonishing repertoire of sounds that includes perfect imitations of other bird species, chainsaws, camera shutters, car alarms, and even human speech. Researchers have documented individual lyrebirds accurately reproducing the songs of more than twenty different bird species in a single performance.

The Wandering Albatross possesses the largest wingspan of any living bird, stretching up to eleven and a half feet from tip to tip. These magnificent seabirds spend the vast majority of their lives soaring over the Southern Ocean, sometimes circling the entire globe without ever touching land. They can fly thousands of miles using a technique called dynamic soaring, which exploits differences in wind speed at various heights above the waves to travel with almost no effort. Wandering Albatrosses mate for life and raise only one chick every two years, performing elaborate dances with their partners that include synchronized bill clacking, wing spreading, and sky pointing.

The Greater Sage-Grouse performs one of the most elaborate mating displays of any bird in North America. Males gather at traditional display grounds called leks before dawn, puffing up their chests to reveal two yellow air sacs, fanning their spiky tail feathers, and producing a series of loud popping and swishing sounds. Females watch from the edges and choose only the most impressive males. These large ground-dwelling birds depend entirely on sagebrush for food and shelter, and their populations have declined as sagebrush habitat has been lost to development and agriculture across the western United States.

The Emperor Penguin endures the harshest breeding conditions of any bird on Earth. After the female lays a single egg in the darkness of Antarctic winter, she transfers it to the male, who balances it on his feet under a warm fold of skin. The male then fasts for over two months in temperatures that can drop below minus 80 degrees Fahrenheit while the female travels to the sea to feed. Males huddle together in groups of thousands, slowly rotating positions so that each penguin gets a turn in the warm center. When the chick hatches, the male feeds it with a special secretion until the female returns from the ocean with food.

New Caledonian Crows are among the most intelligent birds ever studied, displaying problem-solving abilities that rival those of great apes. They are one of the few animals known to craft and use tools in the wild, fashioning hooks from twigs and barbed probes from pandanus leaves to extract insects from crevices. In laboratory settings, they have demonstrated the ability to solve multi-step puzzles, use tools to retrieve other tools, and even plan ahead for future needs. Young crows learn these skills by watching their parents, suggesting a form of cultural transmission that is extremely rare outside of humans and other primates.

The Bar-tailed Godwit holds the record for the longest nonstop flight of any bird, covering over 7,000 miles from Alaska to New Zealand without resting, eating, or drinking. Before departure, these shorebirds nearly double their body weight by storing fat, and their internal organs actually shrink to make room for the extra fuel. The entire journey takes roughly nine days of continuous flight over the open Pacific Ocean with no opportunity to land. Scientists tracked this feat using satellite transmitters and were astonished to confirm that the birds fly directly across the vast expanse of ocean without any stopovers along the way.

The Bowerbird of Australia and New Guinea has one of the most unusual courtship strategies in the animal kingdom. Males spend weeks constructing elaborate structures called bowers from sticks and then decorate them with colorful objects including berries, flowers, shells, feathers, and even human-made items like bottle caps and bits of plastic. Each species has its own preferred colors and architectural style. Satin Bowerbirds favor blue objects, while Great Bowerbirds build hut-like structures with carefully arranged piles of gray and white objects. Males maintain and improve their bowers throughout the breeding season, and females visit multiple bowers before choosing a mate based on the quality of his construction and display.

The Philippine Eagle is one of the largest and most powerful eagles in the world, with a wingspan of over six feet and piercing blue-gray eyes. It is found only in the rainforests of the Philippines and is critically endangered, with fewer than 800 individuals remaining in the wild. Each breeding pair needs up to 25 square miles of old-growth forest to survive, and they raise only one chick every two years. The species faces ongoing threats from deforestation and hunting. In the Philippines, killing a Philippine Eagle carries a penalty of up to twelve years in prison, reflecting the bird's status as the national bird and a symbol of the country's natural heritage.

Honeyguides are small African birds with a remarkable relationship with humans. The Greater Honeyguide actively leads people to wild bee nests by flying ahead, calling loudly, and waiting for them to follow. Once the humans break open the nest and take the honey, the bird feeds on the beeswax and larvae left behind. This cooperative behavior has been documented for centuries and is one of the few known examples of mutualistic communication between wild animals and humans.

The Gentoo Penguin is the fastest swimming bird in the world, reaching speeds of 22 miles per hour underwater. They build nests from small stones and pebbles, and males often present a carefully chosen pebble to a female as a courtship gift. Gentoo colonies can be identified from a distance by the long trails of pink guano that stain the rocky hillsides.

The Andean Condor is one of the heaviest flying birds on Earth, weighing up to 33 pounds with a wingspan over ten feet. It plays a vital role in Andean ecosystems as a scavenger. The Andean Condor holds deep cultural significance across South America and appears on the national coats of arms of several countries.

The Violet-backed Starling of sub-Saharan Africa is one of the most strikingly colored birds in the world. Males have an iridescent violet back that shifts between deep purple and bright blue depending on the angle of light. In contrast, females are brown and streaked, providing excellent camouflage while nesting.

The Red Crossbill has a uniquely shaped bill where the upper and lower mandibles cross over each other. This adaptation allows it to pry open the scales of conifer cones and extract seeds that other birds cannot reach. Different populations have bills of varying sizes, each specialized for a particular type of cone.

The Snowy Egret was hunted nearly to extinction in the late 1800s because its delicate white plumes were prized for decorating women's hats. Public outrage over the slaughter helped launch the modern conservation movement and led to the creation of the first wildlife refuges in the United States.

The Greater Flamingo filters food from water by holding its bill upside down and pumping water through comb-like structures called lamellae. A single flamingo can filter several gallons of water per minute. Flamingo chicks are born white and gradually turn pink over the first two years of their lives as they accumulate pigments from their diet.

The Eurasian Jay buries thousands of acorns each autumn and can remember the locations of most of them months later. This hoarding behavior plays a crucial role in oak forest regeneration, as forgotten acorns sprout into new trees. Jays can also mimic the calls of other birds and even some mechanical sounds.

Wilson's Storm-Petrel is one of the most abundant birds on Earth, yet few people ever see one. These small seabirds spend their entire lives over the open ocean, pattering their feet on the water's surface while hovering to pick up tiny crustaceans. They return to land only to breed in burrows on remote Antarctic islands.

The Satin Bowerbird of eastern Australia collects blue objects with remarkable persistence. Males have been found decorating their bowers with blue bottle caps, pen lids, drinking straws, and even stolen laundry pegs. Older males with the deepest blue-black plumage tend to build the most impressive bowers and attract the most mates.

Mute Swans appear graceful on water but are surprisingly aggressive when defending their nests. They can break a human arm with a blow from their powerful wings. Despite their name, Mute Swans are not truly silent and produce a variety of hissing, grunting, and snorting sounds.

The White-throated Dipper is remarkable for its ability to walk along the bottom of fast-flowing streams to hunt for invertebrates. It has dense waterproof plumage, a low metabolic rate, and a high concentration of oxygen-carrying hemoglobin in its blood, all of which help it survive in icy mountain waters.

The Marabou Stork of Africa stands nearly five feet tall and has a wingspan that can exceed ten feet, making it one of the largest flying birds. Despite its ungainly appearance, it is an efficient scavenger that often feeds alongside vultures. Its bare head and neck are adaptations that help keep it clean while feeding on carrion.

The Lilac-breasted Roller is widely considered one of the most beautiful birds in Africa. It displays at least eight different colors in its plumage. During courtship, males perform dramatic aerial acrobatics, rolling and diving through the sky to impress watching females.

The Long-tailed Tit builds one of the most elaborate nests of any bird. The domed structure is made of moss, lichen, and spider silk, lined with over 1,500 feathers, and takes the pair up to three weeks to complete. The elastic spider silk allows the nest to stretch as the growing chicks need more room.

The Piping Plover is a small, sand-colored shorebird that nests directly on open beaches along the Atlantic coast. Its eggs and chicks are so well camouflaged that beachgoers often walk within inches without noticing them. Conservation efforts include roping off nesting areas and using predator exclosures to protect this endangered species.

The Magnificent Riflebird of northeastern Australia belongs to the bird-of-paradise family. During courtship, the male spreads his glossy black wings wide, throws his head back, and performs a hypnotic swaying dance while opening and closing his wings like a fan. His iridescent throat patch flashes brilliant blue-green in the dappled forest light.

The Helmeted Hornbill of Southeast Asia has a nearly solid casque on top of its bill that makes up about ten percent of its total body weight. Males use these bony helmets in dramatic aerial jousting matches, colliding in midair with loud clacking sounds that echo through the rainforest. Sadly, the bird is critically endangered because its casque is carved into ornaments and traded illegally, sometimes fetching higher prices than elephant ivory on the black market.

The African Grey Parrot is widely regarded as the most intelligent of all parrot species. Studies have shown that these birds can understand concepts like shape, color, number, and even absence. The most famous African Grey, a bird named Alex who was studied for thirty years by researcher Irene Pepperberg, could identify fifty different objects, seven colors, five shapes, and quantities up to six. Alex could also express desires, refuse requests, and combine words in novel ways. He even understood the concept of zero before being taught it, asking what color an object was when shown an empty tray.

The Ivory-billed Woodpecker was once the largest woodpecker in the United States, inhabiting vast old-growth forests of the southeastern swamplands. It was declared extinct in the mid-twentieth century after logging destroyed nearly all of its habitat. However, unconfirmed sightings have periodically sparked hope and controversy among ornithologists. In 2004, a reported sighting in an Arkansas swamp launched a massive search effort involving dozens of researchers, thousands of hours of fieldwork, and millions of dollars. Despite these efforts, no definitive proof of the bird's continued existence has been found, and it remains one of the most debated mysteries in American ornithology.
